meta.title: Parts Manual
meta.versionNote: Draft PDF mapped into a searchable website.

intro.text: Welcome to the ENG 003 parts manual! This site is a cleaned-up, searchable web version of the course parts manual PDF. Use the search bar to find sensors, motors, and example code fast.
Tip: Click any part in the sidebar to jump to it. Each part includes wiring notes and copyable Arduino example code (when provided in the PDF).

intro.title: Original PDF
intro.text: You can open the original PDF any time to see the source layout, diagrams, and page context.

quickNav.title: What are you interested in detecting?

sections.title: Commonly Used Components
sections.description: Motors and displays used frequently in ENG 003.

content.text: DC motors are motors that spin at high speed. To get a DC motor to move in a controlled way with an Arduino, you typically use a motor driver module.
Pinout / terminals
A standard DC motor has two terminals (positive and negative). Swapping the polarity reverses the spin direction.

content.title: Important
content.text: A DC motor should never be powered directly from an Arduino I/O pin. Use a driver and an external power supply sized for the motor, and always share ground between the Arduino and the motor power supply.
Motor driver: L293D

The L293D is a dual H-bridge motor driver IC that can control two DC motors. It uses a separate motor supply (often labeled +12V / VCC2) and a logic supply (+5V / VCC1). The enable pins can be driven with PWM to control speed.
Wiring

rows: Motor A leads → L293D OUT1 / OUT2
Motor B leads → L293D OUT3 / OUT4
content.caption: Arduino ↔ L293D mapping (based on the PDF wiring notes).

content.text: Example code

content.title: Two DC motors with an L293D (from LastMinuteEngineers)

content.text: Stepper motors rotate in discrete steps rather than spinning freely. This makes them useful for precise positioning.
The example in the PDF uses the Arduino Stepper library and a 28BYJ-48 style stepper motor with a small driver board.
Example code

content.title: Stepper motor example (Stepper.h)
Note
content.text: The pin order in the Stepper constructor matters. The PDF notes that the order should be 8, 10, 9, 11 for this motor/driver setup.
This LCD screen is a 16×2 character display with an I2C backpack, so it can be wired with only four connections: power + SDA/SCL.
Pinout
Wiring
I2C is a shared bus, so multiple devices can use the same SDA/SCL wires as long as each device has a unique I2C address.

sections.title: Movement and Location
sections.description: Sensors for distance, motion detection, orientation, and vibration.

content.text: Ultrasonic sensors measure distance by emitting a short ultrasonic pulse (typically 40 kHz) and timing how long it takes for the echo to return.
Pinout

rows: Trigger pin used in the example code
Echo pin used in the example code
content.caption: HC-SR04 pinout used by the example sketch.

content.text: Wiring
Connect VCC to 5V, GND to GND, TRIG to a digital output pin, and ECHO to a digital input pin (matching your code).
Example code

content.title: Ultrasonic distance example

content.text: PIR (passive infrared) sensors detect motion by sensing changes in infrared radiation in their field of view. They typically output HIGH when motion is detected.
Inside the PIR sensor

content.caption: PIR sensor internals / pinout images (source PDF p.26)

content.text: Pinout & wiring

rows: Digital output used in the example code
content.caption: Typical PIR wiring (matches the example sketch).

content.text: Example code

content.title: PIR motion detection example

content.text: The LSM303 combines a 3-axis accelerometer and a 3-axis magnetometer (compass). It communicates via I2C (SDA/SCL).
Wiring
Connect VIN/5V to 5V, GND to ground, and connect SDA/SCL to the Arduino’s I2C pins (UNO: A4/A5; some boards also expose SDA/SCL near AREF).

content.caption: Arduino SDA/SCL pins highlighted (source PDF p.30)

content.title: Libraries
content.text: This example uses Adafruit libraries: Adafruit_Sensor, Adafruit_LSM303_U, and Wire.
Example code

content.title: LSM303 accelerometer + magnetometer example

content.text: A digital tilt sensor behaves like a switch: it outputs HIGH/LOW depending on its orientation (often due to a rolling ball inside).
Wiring
Example code

content.title: Digital tilt sensor example

content.text: Force Sensitive Resistors (FSRs) change resistance depending on how much force is applied. They’re usually read using a voltage divider into an analog input.
Pinout / wiring concept
Example code

content.title: FSR analog threshold example

content.text: A piezo-based vibration sensor outputs an analog signal that increases with vibration or impacts.
Wiring
The PDF wiring notes reference pins labeled “NC”, “-”, and “S”: connect NC to 5V, “-” to ground, and “S” to an analog input.

content.caption: Vibration sensor wiring diagram (source PDF p.42)

content.text: Example code

content.title: Vibration sensor analog read example

sections.title: Environmental Sensing
sections.description: Gas, dust, water, temperature, humidity, UV, and related environmental sensors.

content.text: Gas sensors like the MQ series work by changing resistance in the presence of target gases. Many MQ sensors require a warm-up period (several minutes) before readings stabilize.
Reading the sensor

The sensor is typically used in a voltage divider with a load resistance (RL). Using the measured voltage, you can estimate the sensor resistance (Rs). The PDF notes the relationship: Rs = RL * (Vin/Vout - 1).

content.title: Warm-up
content.text: The PDF notes a 3–5 minute warm-up time for this type of sensor.
Example code

content.title: MQ-135 multi-gas example (as provided in the PDF)

content.text: The MS1100 VOC sensor is similar in concept to the MQ-135: it has a resistive sensor that requires warm-up and provides analog + digital outputs.
Unlike the MQ-135, the PDF notes it does not detect carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide, or ammonium. It is sensitive to formaldehyde, toluene, benzene, alcohol, and methane.
The PDF suggests calibrating by recording the resistance in clean air and comparing future readings to that baseline.

content.caption: MS1100 VOC sensor section (source PDF p.51)

content.text: This optical dust sensor uses an internal light source and detector to estimate dust concentration. The example code prints an estimated concentration in ug/m³.
Example code

content.title: Dust sensor example (WaveShare)

content.text: The DHT11 provides digital temperature and humidity readings. The PDF links to an Adafruit guide for wiring details.

content.title: Library
content.text: Install the DHT11 library (DHT11.h) to use the example sketch.
Example code

content.title: DHT11 temperature + humidity example

content.text: Reference guide (from the PDF):
The rain sensor uses a capacitive plate to detect moisture. The module provides a digital output (DO) with adjustable threshold and an analog output (AO) that varies with wetness.
The PDF also notes the plate may include a heating element so droplets dry faster and readings reflect current rain intensity more directly.
Example code (analog output)

content.title: Rain sensor range-mapping example

content.text: Capacitive soil moisture sensors estimate how wet soil is. The PDF notes the sensor is not highly precise and should be calibrated to your use case.

content.caption: Soil moisture sensor wiring/photo (source PDF p.61)

content.text: Example code (air/water calibration)

content.title: Soil moisture calibration example
Calibration
content.text: Update AirValue and WaterValue after taking readings in air and in water, then adjust thresholds to fit your project.
These capacitive water sensors produce a signal when water contacts the long conductive traces on the sensor.
Example code

content.title: Water level sensor example

content.text: Flow sensors measure flow rate by counting pulses from a small turbine/propeller. The sensor outputs pulses at a rate proportional to flow.
Example code

content.title: Flow rate example (interrupt-based)
Note
content.text: The PDF includes two attachInterrupt() styles. Use ONE of them (the 'digitalPinToInterrupt' form is recommended on many boards).
A TDS sensor estimates how many solids are dissolved in water, typically reported in parts per million (ppm).
Example code

content.title: TDS meter example

content.text: Reference links included in the PDF:
One-wire submerged temperature sensors output digital data over a OneWire bus. The PDF uses the OneWire and DallasTemperature libraries.
Example code

content.title: OneWire temperature example

content.text: Ambient temperature sensors measure air temperature around the sensor and output a voltage that can be read with an Arduino analog input (A0–A5).
The PDF highlights the TMP36 as a common analog temperature sensor used in this class.
Pinout
With the flat side facing you and the legs pointing downward, the pins are: left = 2.7–5.5V in, middle = analog voltage out, right = ground (as shown in the PDF).

content.title: Draft note
content.text: The PDF draft shows the TMP36 pinout but does not include an example sketch in this section.

sections.title: Human Sensing and Controls
sections.description: Buttons, touch, rotary encoders, keypads, and other input devices.

content.title: Draft note
content.text: The PDF includes a 'Buttons' and 'Push Button' heading without additional wiring/code in the provided draft pages.
The PDF links to an external tutorial for a touch sensor.
A rotary encoder produces pulses as it turns, allowing you to detect direction and count steps. Many modules also include a push button (SW).
Example code

content.title: Rotary encoder counter example

content.text: A keypad is a matrix of push buttons indexed by row and column. The example code scans the rows/columns and reports key events in the serial monitor.

content.title: Library
content.text: Install the 'Adafruit Keypad' library from the Arduino Library Manager.
